content.assessment: This proposal has the strongest structural rationale but the weakest political feasibility profile in the set. The 6/8-year tracks emerged from a cross-ideological 1989-90 coalition combining church and liberal interests, meaning full delay-to-14 reform would need to dismantle a settlement with deep symbolic roots. Empirically, even a much narrower administrative review was reframed by opposition as a 'unified four-year model' with communist-era connotations, indicating this proposal's scope would almost certainly trigger an intense and probably successful resistance campaign, especially from the government's own signaled reassurance to denominational-school stakeholders.
opportunities: Cross-country evidence of comparable single-structure reforms could be marshaled to build technocratic legitimacy if political conditions shifted
strengths: Directly targets the mechanism most associated with inequality by eliminating both early entry points, giving it the clearest normative justification of any option under this lens
threats: High reversal risk: even if enacted, a change of government or coalition pressure from church/middle-class constituencies could roll back reform, given the enduring political salience of the 1989-90 settlement [F-live-political_feasibility-01]
Opposition's communism-era framing raises the risk that any narrower version of this policy gets rhetorically escalated into full-scale political conflict [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
weaknesses: Runs directly against the founding cross-ideological coalition logic (church and liberal interests) that created and has sustained the tracks since 1989-90 [F-live-political_feasibility-01]

A far narrower proposal (admissions consultation) was already reframed as this exact 'unified four-year model,' evidencing that the full version would face amplified opposition [F-live-political_feasibility-04]

Government has already moved to reassure church-school stakeholders that abolition is not intended, signaling this proposal contradicts current governing coalition's declared position [F-live-political_feasibility-05]
Teaching profession lacks unified support, weakening any pro-reform coalition PSZ could offer [F-live-political_feasibility-06]
